coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots boost microbial electrosynthesis via efficient extracellular electron uptake of acetogens
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Jiaping
Zeng, Cuiping
Liu, Guangli
Luo, Haiping
coredata.dc:description: This study investigated the molecular mechanism behind the highly efficient performance of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs)-assisted microbial electrosynthesis systems (MESs). The impact of NCDs (C:N precursor = 1:0.5–1:3) on acetogens was examined in the biocathode. The highest electrocatalytic performance was observed with NCDs1:1. The maximum acetate production rate of 1.9 ± 0.1 mM d−1 was achieved in NCDs1:1 -modified MESs, which was 26.7–216.7 % higher than other MESs (0.6–1.5 mM d−1). With NCDs1:1 modified, the biocathode exhibited a 129.3–186.8 % increase in the abundance of Sporomusa, and 38.5–104.6 % increase in cytochrome expression (cydAB, cybH). Transcriptome confirmed that cytochromes played a crucial role in the extracellular electron uptake (EEU) of NCDs1:1-modified Sporomusa. NCDs1:1 enhanced EEU efficiency, thereby increasing the two H+-pumping steps and accelerating microbial CO2 fixation. These results provide valuable insights into increasing CO2 fixation by maximizing EEU efficiency in acetogens.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microbial electrosynthesis
Nitrogen doped carbon dots
Sporomusa
Extracellular electron transfer
Transcriptome

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: NEW AND EMERGING BIOMASS-BASED* PROCESSES AND TECHNOLOOGIES

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of NCDs and electrochemical measurement
Microbial community composition and functional prediction
Response of MESs performance to the electrocatalytic activity of NCDs
Selective enrichment of Sporomusa and functional prediction
Transcriptional analysis for EEU mechanism of NCDs-modified Sporomusa
Cytochrome expression of S. ovata-MESs induced by NCDs
Central metabolism of S. ovata-MESs enhanced by NCDs
Hypothesis of EEU mechanisms in NCDs-modified acetogens

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 42077286), the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (Nos. 2023A1515011846, 2023A1515030212). We thank Dr. Yifeng Zhang for the valuable discussions.

This work was financially supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 42077286 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. 2023A1515011846 ), the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (No. 2023A1515030212 ). We thank Dr. Yifeng Zhang for the valuable discussions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Electrocatalytic activity of NCDsX (X = 1:0.5–1:3) coated GCE in N2-saturated 0.10 M KCl solution with K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] (both 1.0 mM) as redox probe. (A) CV curve; (B) Correlation analysis between the content of nitrogen precursor and oxidation peak current (Ipa)/reduction peak current (Ipc) of CV curve in NCDsX; (C, D) Nyquist plots and total resistance value analyzed by EIS.

The performance of NCDsX (X = 1:0.5–1:3) modified biocathode-MESs (CK: without modification). (A) Maximum current and coulombs; (B) acetate accumulation and electron recovery efficiency; (C) correlation matrix analysis between MESs performance and oxidation peak current (Ipa) of CV curve in NCDsX. Pearson's coefficient (r) > 0.7 indicated significant correlation at p < 0.05 (double tail).

Cathodic microbial community and functional prediction of CK, NCDs1:1 and NCDs1:3 in the biocathode-MESs. (A) The distribution of dominant genera (top 9); (B) heatmap analysis of the top 25 of cytochrome- related functional genes according to high-throughput sequencing function prediction and KEGG database by PICRUSt2; (C) redundancy analysis of the relationship among dominant genera (dashed line, black), cytochrome-related functional genes (dashed line, black) and active N species (solid line, red).

(A) Volcano plot analysis for the differential gene expression of NCDs1:1-modified S. ovata vs. unmodified S. ovata in MESs at the cathodic potential of −0.4 V (vs. SHE); (B) heatmap analysis for cytochrome expression differences of NCDs1:1-modified S. ovata vs. unmodified S. ovata in MESs at the cathodic potential of −0.4 V (vs. SHE); (C) the expression level of cydAB and cybH in S. ovata-MESs at the cathodic potential of −0.4 V and −0.7 V (vs. SHE), respectively. (Fold change represents the value of differential folds).

Transcriptional analysis for NCDs1:1-modified S. ovata vs. unmodified S. ovata in MESs at the cathodic potential of −0.4 V (vs. SHE): (A) the expression of central metabolism-related genes, (B) the Wood-Ljungdahl pathway for carbon fixation, (C) a H+-dependent ferredoxin, NAD+ oxidoreductase (Rnf) complex-dependent membrane bound respiratory chain and (D) a cytochrome/quinone-dependent membrane bound respiratory chain for energy conversion (red font represented upregulated expression of genes in NCDs1:1-modified S. ovata).

Proposed schematic illustration of EEU in NCDs-modified Sporomusa strain (red font represented upregulated expression of genes and the enhancement of central metabolism; Rnf, Stn, bif. Hyd, cyt, Fd, Q and QH2 are H+- dependent ferredoxin:NAD+ oxidoreductase, ferredoxin-dependent NADH:NADP+ oxidoreductase, electron-bifurcating hydrogenase, cytochrome, ferredoxin, ubiquinone, and ubiquinol, respectively; MTHFR is methylene-THF reductase in the Wood-Ljungdahl pathway; undefined membrane-bound electron carriers are denoted by '?').
The proportion analysis of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N based on XPS full survey in different samples.

Upregulated genes encoding cytochrome in S. ovata-MESs with NCDs1:1 modification (protein properties including number of heme-binding sites, number of tansmembrane helices, and subcellular localization prediction were shown; bold font represented that cytochrome may be located in the outer membrane).

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Heme-binding sites based on searching CXnCH (n = 2–5) motifs.
Number of transmembrane helices predicted by TMHMM.
Subcellular localization predicted by PSORTb v.3.0.2.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots boost microbial electrosynthesis via efficient extracellular electron uptake of acetogens

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control and Remediation Technology, School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control and Remediation Technology
School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control and Remediation Technology, School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Quantitative Engineering Biology, Shenzhen Institute of Synthetic Biology, Shenzhen Institute of Advanced Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shenzhen 518055, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Quantitative Engineering Biology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Quantitative Engineering Biology, Shenzhen Institute of Synthetic Biology, Shenzhen Institute of Advanced Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shenzhen 518055, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigated the molecular mechanism behind the highly efficient performance of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs)-assisted microbial electrosynthesis systems (MESs). The impact of NCDs (C:N precursor = 1:0.5–1:3) on acetogens was examined in the biocathode. The highest electrocatalytic performance was observed with NCDs1:1. The maximum acetate production rate of 1.9 ± 0.1 mM d−1 was achieved in NCDs1:1 -modified MESs, which was 26.7–216.7 % higher than other MESs (0.6–1.5 mM d−1). With NCDs1:1 modified, the biocathode exhibited a 129.3–186.8 % increase in the abundance of Sporomusa, and 38.5–104.6 % increase in cytochrome expression (cydAB, cybH). Transcriptome confirmed that cytochromes played a crucial role in the extracellular electron uptake (EEU) of NCDs1:1-modified Sporomusa. NCDs1:1 enhanced EEU efficiency, thereby increasing the two H+-pumping steps and accelerating microbial CO2 fixation. These results provide valuable insights into increasing CO2 fixation by maximizing EEU efficiency in acetogens.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Microbial electrosynthesis systems (MESs), as one of the most desirable and sustainable biotechnology, can realize CO2 fixation through the interaction between electroactive microorganisms and cathode (Jiang et al., 2018; Nevin et al., 2010). The core of MESs lies in the ability of a microbe to uptake electrons from solid-state electron donors (e.g., carbon electrode or Fe minerals), which is known as extracellular electron uptake (EEU) (Philips, 2019). EEU has been observed in multiple species that are widely distributed in natural habitats, such as acetogens (Philips, 2019), methanogens (Palacios et al., 2024), and iron oxidizers (Bose et al., 2014; Gupta et al., 2019). For example, Sporomusa ovata was first found to reduce CO2 to acetate in MESs with a cathode potential of −0.4 V (Nevin et al., 2010). S. ovata/CdS hybrid system could efficiently harvest light energy and transfer photogenerated electrons to efficiently synthesize acetate from CO2 under illumination conditions (He et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2024). Thus, MESs have the advantages of abundant availability, room temperature operation, self-regeneration, and low cost (Jiang et al., 2018), as compared with the traditional electrochemical reduction using precious metal catalysts. However, the application of MESs is still challenging because of the low EEU efficiency (Bian et al., 2024).

Previous studies have proposed that conductive materials can improve the electron transfer rate between the cells and the electrode (Cheng et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2015). The nanomaterials such as quantum dots may scattered inside and outside of microbial cells and participate in the process of transmembrane extracellular electron transfer (EET), thereby increasing electron utilization efficiency. For example, carbon dots (CDs) can enhance electron transfer from cells to solid electron acceptor (i.e. outward EET) in Shewanella by triggering the cellular physiological response to secrete abundant electron shuttles (Yang et al., 2020). N-doped Fe3O4@CDs could enhance outward EET in Geobacter by forming an interaction network with conductive proteins inside and outside Geobacter (Cheng et al., 2022). Intracellular N, Fe-doped CDs could enhance the activities of the electron transport system, thereby improving carbon/nitrogen/sulfur metabolism (Li et al., 2024). In addition, N-doped nanomaterials could enhance cytochrome-mediated outward EET in Shewanella (Yu et al., 2015). However, the above works mainly focused on the outward EET process and rarely investigated inward EET (i.e. EEU).

Recent study found that internalized N-doped CDs (NCDs) may accelerate the EEU of mixed-culture acetogens in MESs by acting as an “electron assistant”, thereby promoting microbial electrosynthesis of acetate (Hu et al., 2023). It was reported that N-doped materials can promote the outward EET efficiency in Shewanella by interacting with OmcA/MtrC proteins (Wang et al., 2020), and anchoring the diffused electron shuttle on the anodic interface (Wu et al., 2020). However, the molecular response mechanism of acetogens to NCDs materials is still unclear. On the other hand, three different types of species, pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, would be introduced into the CDs during N doping. N dopant states can regulate the electrocatalytic activity of materials, and the dominant species may vary in the different systems (Wang et al., 2020; Haider et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2023). For example, pyrrolic N was the main active N for improving bioelectricity generation in bioelectrochemical systems (Wang et al., 2020). Pyridinic N was the key active site for boosting hydroxyl radical yield in an Electro-Fenton-like system (Haider et al., 2023). Graphitic N was responsible for the high-efficiency synthesis of hydrogen peroxide in an electrocatalytic system (Wang et al., 2023). The roles of different active N species in the EEU process of electro-autotrophic microbes are very limited so far.

In this study, a series of NCDs with varying molar ratios of carbon and nitrogen precursor were prepared to evaluate the response of acetogenic biocathode-MES performance to electrocatalytic activity of NCDs. To further explore how NCDs affected the EEU process of acetogens, S. ovata was used as a research object to investigate its transcriptional expression differences. S. ovata is one of the typical acetogens that is widely distributed in natural environments and has been proven to have EEU ability. This work would provide some insights into understanding the carbon cycling that is driven by the EEU process of acetogens.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NCDs and electrochemical measurement
ce:section.ce:para: The NCDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method according to previous research (Hu et al., 2023). To regulate the active N species of NCDs, the molar ratios of citric acid and urea were set to 1:0.5, 1:0.75, 1:1, 1:1.25, 1:1.5, 1:1.75, 1:1.2 and 1:3, respectively. Correspondingly, NCDs synthesized at a specific molar ratio (x) were labeled as NCDsx. Eventually, NCDsx solutions with a concentration range of 121.8–153.9 g/L were obtained. The electrical properties of NCDsx were measured by loading NCDsx on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE), with a load of 0.5 mg cm−2. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed for the NCDsx-coated GCE using an electrochemical workstation (Donghua, Shanghai). The EIS measurements were carried out from 1 × 105 to 0.05 Hz at open-circuit potential with 5 mV amplitude. Data from EIS were obtained as Nyquist plots and fitted to an equivalent electrical circuit (RQ((RW)) (QR)) using ZsimDemo analysis software. CV and EIS measurements of the NCDsx-loaded GCE were performed in multiple cycles to obtain reliable data. The electrochemical measurements were conducted under anaerobic conditions, and the most representative data are presented.

The mixed-culture acetogens were enriched from the sediment of the Pearl River (Guangzhou, China) using DSM 879 medium (adjusted 2.0 g/L NaHCO3, 0.1 g/L yeast extract; omitted D-fructose, resazurin sodium, cysteine and sodium sulfide) in anaerobic bottle. The H2-CO2 mixture (80/20, v/v) was supplied into the anaerobic bottle for 10 min every 24 h, with 4 d as a cycle. Sub-cultured acetogens for more than one year were inoculated into the cathode chamber of MES.

S. ovata (DSM-2662) was purchased from Deutsche Sammlung Mikroorganismen und Zellkulturen (DSMZ, Braunschweig, Germany). S. ovata was routinely cultivated in the complete DSM 311medium, and then was transferred at least three times in 100 mL anaerobic bottles under a N2-CO2 (80:20, v/v) atmosphere before conducting the subsequent S. ovata-MES experiments. All anaerobic bottles were placed on a constant temperature shaker (30 °C, 180 r min−1) for batch culture.

The dual-chamber glass reactor was used as biocathode-MES, with a Nafion 117 proton exchange membrane (DuPont, USA) as the separator between the anode and cathode chamber. The anode and cathode were each composed of 5 graphite plates (length × width × thickness = 5 cm × 2 cm × 0.5 cm). The working volume of the anodic and cathodic chamber of MES was about 130 mL. The mixture of acetogens and NCDsX (200 mg/L) was inoculated into the cathode chamber. The cathodic potential was set at −0.7 V (vs. SHE) using a multi-channel potentiostat (CHI1000C, CH Instruments, Inc., China). During the first 0–6 d of MESs operation, the H₂-CO₂ mixture (80/20, v/v) was sparged into the cathodic chamber for 10 min every 48 h to speed up the enrichment of acetogens on the cathode surface. After refreshing the catholyte and anolyte on day 6, the H₂-CO₂ was replaced with the N₂-CO₂ mixture (80/20, v/v). The MESs ran for about 25 d with a cycle of 4 d, during which pure CO2 gas was sparged into the cathodic chamber for 2 min every day to alleviate the pH change of the catholyte. After each aeration, 2.5 mL of acetylene was reinjected into the top of the cathodic chamber or anaerobic bottle to inhibit methane production.

For S. ovata-MESs operation, the above-mentioned MES reactor was used, but the graphite plate cathode was replaced with the carbon cloth cathode (64 cm2). The purpose of using the carbon cloth cathode was to provide higher surface area for cells attachment and obtain sufficient biomass for transcriptional analyses. The S. ovata-MESs were inoculated with bacterial cells at an initial OD600 of 0.1 and incubated at 30 °C. Subsequently, three operational stages in the S. ovata-MESs were performed to further increase the effective biomass, respectively. The DSM-adjusted 311 medium was used for S. ovata-MESs (see supplementary material). All anaerobic medium for pure bacteria was sterilized by autoclaving at 121 °C. NaHCO3, vitamin and trace element were added from sterile stocks after autoclaving.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microbial community composition and functional prediction
ce:section.ce:para: At the end of mixed-culture MESs operation, each graphite plate was scraped with a sterile blade. The scraped biofilm was then combined for analyzing the composition of the cathodic microbial community using three generations of full-length sequencing technology. The full-length bacterial 16S rRNA gene sequencing process and the microbial sequencing data analysis were performed according to previous research (Hu et al., 2023). The expression of cytochrome-related functional genes in the cathodic microbial community was predicted by PICRUSt2. Moreover, the chemical composition on the surface of NCDsX-modified biocathode (5 mm × 5 mm) was characterized by XPS (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, UK). The XPS data of N 1s peaks were analyzed by Gauss fitting in software (Avantage 4.15) to identify the type of active N species in cathodic biofilm. Redundancy analysis among functional gene, active N species and acetogens in cathodic biofilm was performed to investigate the relationship between NCDs and acetogens.

High-quality total RNA was isolated using the RNeasy PowerSoil Total RNA Kit reagents (Qiagen, Hilden, Germany) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. RNA sequencing was performed by Majorbio Bio-Pharm Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). RNA-seq transcriptome libraries were constructed using the Illumina TruSeq™ RNA Sample Preparation Kit (San Diego, CA). RNA-seq of S. ovata, with and without NCDs modification, generated a total of 0.24 to 0.26 million sequence reads mapped to the reference genome of S. ovata DSM 2662 (NCBI accession ASXP00000000.1), with at least 68 % genome mapped ratio. These results indicated that the selected reference genome was appropriate and that pure bacteria related experiments were not contaminated. The RNA-seq data were visualized at the Majorbio Cloud platform (cloud.majorbio.com). The main methods were as follows: RSEM was used to quantify gene expression; the TPM (Transcripts Per Million reads) method was used to calculated expression levels; differential expression between groups was identified using DEGseq software with default parameters: p < 0.05 and |log2 FC| ≥ 1.

The extraction method for components distributed inside and outside bacterial cells was briefly described as follows (Hu et al., 2023): 10 mL cell suspension was centrifuged at 10,000 × g for 10 min. The pellet was resuspended in 0.9 % NaCl solution and incubated at 55 °C for 30 min. After centrifugation at 8000 × g for 15 min, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22-μm filter and termed the 'extracellular component'. The remaining cells were resuspended in 0.2 N NaOH with sterile glass beads, vortexed for 5 min, and then centrifuged at 12,000 × g for 6 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22-μm filter and termed the 'intracellular component'. Fluorescence spectra of these components were recorded using a FluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrometer (HORIBA, USA). Cytochrome c in the extracellular component was characterized using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600i, Shimadzu, Japan). The morphology of NCDs was observed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2010HR, Japan) at 200 kV. Ion chromatography equipped with an anion IC column (CIC-D100, Shenghan, China) was used to determine acetate concentration. The electron recovery efficiency for acetate production was calculated using the formula bnF/Q, where b is the number of electron (8 electrons per mole of acetate), n is the moles of acetate production, F is the constant of Faraday (96,485C·mol−1), and Q is the integral of current over time (C). Unless otherwise specified, all samples were tested in triplicate, and error bars represent the standard deviations calculated from these measurements. Sequence data were deposited in the National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI) website under BioProject PRJNA1149173.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the CV oxidation peak current (Ipa) and reduction peak current (Ipc) of NCDsX exhibited a strong correlation with the content of nitrogen precursor (R2 = 0.80–0.95) (Fig. 1A, B), indicating that the content of nitrogen precursor was the key factor regulating the electrocatalytic activity of NCDs. In addition, CV tests showed that the electrocatalytic activity of NCDsX initially increased and subsequently decreased with the increasing content of nitrogen precursor, which was consistent with the results of EIS fitting (Fig. 1C, D). The electrocatalytic activity of carbon dots can be significantly enhanced by doping an appropriate content of nitrogen precursors (Stachurski et al., 2020), but excessive dopant may result in the collapse of the carbon core.

Electrochemical tests showed that NCDs1:1 and NCDs1:3 exhibited the highest and lowest electrocatalytic properties, respectively. The CV peak current of NCDs1:1 was 229.4 % higher than that of NCDs1:3. The total resistance value of NCDs1:1 was 63.8 % lower than that of NCDs1:3. The electrocatalytic differences in N-doped materials can be attributed to the varying proportion of active N species, including pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N (Wang et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2023). Based on the results of CV and EIS, NCDs1:1 and NCDs1:3 were selected as representative NCDs for further functional group analysis in this study. The results shown that pyrrolic N was dominant in NCDs1:1 and NCDs1:3, with relative contents of 5.6 % and 4.5 %, respectively, followed by graphitic N and pyridinic N with the relative content from 1.9 % to 2.5 % (Table 1 ). The sp3 hybridized pyrrolic N in a five-membered ring contributes two p electrons to the π system, which can improve the electrocatalytic performance of N-doped materials by changing their electronic structure and increasing active sites (Stachurski et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2021). Therefore, pyrrolic N may be an important factor leading to their electrochemical differences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of MESs performance to the electrocatalytic activity of NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of NCDsX (X = 1:0.5–1:3) modified acetogens on MESs performance is shown in Fig. 2 . The performance of MESs, including maximum current and coulombs, increased with the electrocatalytic activity of NCDsX (X = 1:0.5–1:1) and decreased with the electrocatalytic activity of NCDsX (X = 1:1–1:3). The highest values of maximum current and coulombs were 9.6 ± 0.3 mA and 1086.0 ± 19.7 C in NCDs1:1-modified MESs, which were 58.4–77.8 % higher than those of CK (without modification) (Fig. 2A). The lowest values of maximum current and coulombs were 3.0 ± 0.2 mA and 495.9 ± 52.1 C in NCDs1:3-modified MESs, which were 27.7–44.4 % lower than those of CK (Fig. 2A). The significant electrocatalytic difference between NCDs1:1- and NCD1:3- modified MESs may be attributed to different proportion of active N species in the cathodic biofilm (Table 1). In the abiotic MESs with NCDsX addition directly, the maximum current and coulombs were only 0.4–0.7 mA and 27.1–64.9 C (see supplementary material), which were significantly lower than those in the biocathode-MESs (Fig. 2A). Previous study reported that NCDs in the catholyte did not enhance the generation of cathodic current, and the improved performance of MES mainly depended on NCDs-mediated cathodic biofilm (Hu et al., 2023). Additionally, the background current caused by CDs and/or CDs- modified dead cells can be ignored (Liu et al., 2020). The results indicated that the electrochemical differences among the biocathode-MESs could be attributed to living cells that modified with NCDsX.

The acetate synthesis efficiency in NCDsX (X = 1:0.5–1:3) modified biocathode-MESs is shown in Fig. 2B. The average acetate production rate of the CK was ∼1.0 mM d−1, which is comparable with previous studies (∼1.2 mM d−1), indicating the successful start-up of the MESs in this study (Hu et al., 2023). Acetate accumulation increased with the increasing electrocatalytic activity of NCDsX (X = 1:0.5–1:1). The highest value of 7.7 ± 0.2 mM was obtained with NCDs1:1, which was 92.5 % higher than that of CK (∼4.0 mM). The maximum acetate production rate of 1.9 ± 0.1 mM d−1 was obtained with NCDs1:1, which was 26.7–216.7 % higher than that of other NCDsX (0.6–1.5 mM d−1). With NCDsX (X = 1:1.25–1:3) modified acetogens, the acetate accumulation in MESs was 2.9–3.3 mM, which was 17.5–27.5 % lower than that of CK. Notably, for mixed-culture acetogens under H2:CO2 cultivation, acetate accumulation had no difference with and without NCDsx modification (29.2–31.7 mM), and no correlation with the electrocatalytic activity of NCDx (X = 1:0.5–1:3) (r = 0.04) (see supplementary material). These results indicated that the NCDx cannot be internalized as a carbon source for acetogens, but instead improved the performance of the MES by affecting the electron transfer between bacteria and the cathode.

For electron recovery efficiency for acetate production, NCDs1:1-modified biocathode-MESs obtained the highest value of 82.4 ± 1.0 %, which was 21.0 % higher than that of CK (68.1 ± 1.4 %) (Fig. 2B). There was no significant difference between NCDs1:3 and CK. Pearson correlation analysis showed that the maximum current, coulombs, and acetate accumulation had a strong positive correlation with the electrocatalytic activity of NCDsX (X = 1:0.5–1:3), with correlation coefficients of 0.80, 0.72, and 0.69, respectively (Fig. 2C). However, the electron recovery efficiency of biocathode-MESs was weakly correlated with the electrocatalytic activity of NCDsX (X = 1:0.5–1:3) (r = 0.22). Previous studies demonstrated that the EET efficiency of electroactive microbes could be improved by the strengthened interaction between the conductive material and electron transport component (Zhang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2020). Carbon nanomaterials can participate in transmembrane extracellular electron transfer processes, thereby increasing electron utilization efficiency (Cheng et al., 2022; Li et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2015). Therefore, the electron recovery efficiency may depend on the interaction between specific active N species in NCDs and particular electron transport components in acetogens, which will be discussed later.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective enrichment of Sporomusa and functional prediction
ce:section.ce:para: The distribution of microbial community, the predicted abundance of functional genes and the proportion of different active N species in cathodic biofilm were further analyzed to investigate the relationship between NCDs and acetogens. As shown in Fig. 3 A, 9 genera accounted for 84.6–89.4 % of microbial community composition in CK, NCDs1:1 and NCDs1:3, among which Sporomusa, Acetobacterium and Acetoanaerobium explained acetate production in the biocathode-MESs. Compared with CK, the relative abundance of acetogens increased by 22.0 % with NCDs1:1 modification and decreased by 35.7 % with NCDs1:3 modification, which explained the differences in acetate production between NCDs1:1 and NCDs1:3. Sporomusa was dominant in NCDs1:1, and its relative abundance was 129.3–186.8 % higher than that in CK and NCDs1:3. Acetoanaerobium was dominant in CK, and its relative abundance was 72.0––155.6 % higher than that in NCDs1:1 and NCDs1:3. Generally, Acetobacterium belongs to the Na+-dependent acetogens and lacks cytochromes, while Sporomusa belongs to the H+-dependent acetogens and contains cytochromes (Rosenbaum and Müller, 2021).

According to the literature, most of the acetogens with EEU capacity belong to Sporomusa (Philips, 2019). Since cytochromes are important components in the membrane-bound electron transport chain, the expression of the top 25 cytochrome-related genes were predicted by PICRUSt2 (Fig. 3B). The results indicated that cydAB encoding cytochrome bd and cybH encoding cytochrome b were selectively enriched in NCDs1:1-modified biofilm, and their expression was upregulated by 38.5–104.6 % compared with CK and NCDs1:3. Pyrrolic N occupied in the NCDs1:1-modified biofilm, and its proportion increased by 121.7–131.8 % compared to CK and NCDs1:3, indicating that pyrrolic N was introduced by NCDs1:1 into the cathodic biofilm and probably plays an important role in electron transport (Table 1). The redundancy analysis showed that pyrrolic N had a strong positive correlation not only with Sporomusa but also with cytochrome-related genes (cydAB and cybH) (Fig. 3C). Moreover, these genes are present in the genomes of extracellularly respiring acetogens such as Sporomusa ovata, Sporomusa silvacetica, and Sporomusa sphaeroides. Previous studies proposed that pyrrolic N can easily absorb electrons kinetically from porphyrin-iron active centers of cytochrome c. Pyrrolic N also boosted the direct EET process of Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 by facilitating interaction between OmcA/MtrC protein and the anodic interface (Wang et al., 2020). Furthermore, a positive correlation was observed between the content of pyrrolic N and the redox activity of cytochrome c (Wang et al., 2020). Therefore, it is reasonable to believe that the introduction of pyrrolic N into cathodic biofilm by NCDs1:1 may induce the expression of cytochrome-related genes (cydAB and cybH) in Sporomusa, thereby improving the overall performance of acetogenic biocathode-MESs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transcriptional analysis for EEU mechanism of NCDs-modified Sporomusa
Cytochrome expression of S. ovata-MESs induced by NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that cytochrome c plays a crucial role in outward EET. In this study, 10 upregulated genes encoding cytochrome c are not homologous to mtrABC and omcS involving in the outward EET of Shewanella spp. and Geobacter spp. (Igarashi and Kato, 2021), suggesting that the EEU of acetogens has a different electron transfer mechanism as compared with the outward EET. Heme, the active center of cytochrome c, is essential for the formation of a direct electron transfer pathway across the inner membrane, periplasmic space, and outer membrane (Chong et al., 2018). Cytochrome c encoding genes SOV_2c11600, SOV_2c11690, SOV_6c01070 and SOV_2c11700 contain at least four heme-binding motifs (Table 2). Although subcellular localization prediction did not identify extracellular multi-heme cytochrome c in the genome of S. ovata, UV–vis absorption spectra of extracellular component from S. ovata suggested the possible presence of cytochrome c in the outer membrane (see supplementary material). With NCDs1:1 modification, the enhanced characteristic peak of cytochrome c was consistent with the upregulated expression of genes encoding multi-heme cytochrome c (see supplementary material and Fig. 4B). The multi-heme cytochrome c encoding genes SOV_2c11600 and SOV_2c11690 were predicted to have unknown locations and may therefore be critical components of the EEU in S. ovata (Table 2). These results indicated that (multi-heme) cytochrome c plays an indispensable role in the EEU of NCDs1:1-modified S. ovata.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Central metabolism of S. ovata-MESs enhanced by NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Almost all genes related to carbon fixation, energy conversion, and ATP synthesis were upregulated in NCDs1:1-modified MESs at the cathodic potential of −0.4 V (vs. SHE) (Fig. 5 A). The genes encoding various enzymes and cofactors involved in Wood-Ljungdahl pathway (WLP), including the formate dehydrogenase (fdh), the formyl-THF synthase (fhs), the methenyl-THF cyclohydrolase (fchA), the methylene-THF dehydrogenase (folD), the methylene-THF reductase (MTHFR: mvhD, metVF, and hdrCBA), the CODH/ACS (acsA and acsB), the corrinoid/iron sulfur protein (acsCD), the corrinoid activation and regeneration protein (acsV), the CoFeSP methyltransferase (acsE), the CODH maturation factor (cooC) and the CODH nickel-insertion protein (acsF) (Ragsdale and Pierce, 2008). Almost all of these genes in WLP were upregulated by NCDs1:1, which together promoted the conversion of CO2 to acetyl-CoA. Concurrently, the upregulated expression of acetate kinase encoding gene ack and phosphotransacetylase encoding gene pta promoted the conversion of acetyl-CoA to acetate (Fig. 5B). In fact, net ATP synthesis by substrate-level phosphorylation in the WLP is zero, and thus an additional chemiosmotic mechanism of ATP synthesis must exist in acetogens for bacterial growth (Schuchmann and Müller, 2014, 2016). The ATP synthase encoding gene atpIBFHAGDC was upregulated by NCDs1:1 (Fig. 5A), suggesting that NCDs1:1 modification enhanced the metabolic energy supply of electro-autotrophic S. ovata. These results indicated that NCDs1:1 modification may improve acetate production in Sporomusa-dominated biofilms by enhancing the expression of genes involved in CO2 fixation and ATP synthesis.

So far, two membrane-bound respiratory chains, a Rnf complex-dependent respiratory chain and a cytochrome/quinone-dependent respiratory chain, have been proposed for chemoautotrophic S. ovata converting energy (Kremp et al., 2020; Kremp et al., 2022). During the energy conversion process, the upregulation of genes encoding electron-bifurcating hydrogenase (bif. Hyd) complex (hydCEDBA), ferredoxin:NAD+ oxidoreductase (Rnf) complex (rnfDGEA), and ferredoxin-dependent NADH:NADP+ oxidoreductase (Stn) complex (stnABC) was observed in electro-autotrophic S. ovata with NCDs1:1 modification (Fig. 5A). In the Rnf complex- dependent membrane-bound respiratory chain, electrons are transported via various electron carriers, such as NAD+, NADP+, and/or ferredoxin (Fd) (Fig. 5C). Firstly, electrons are transferred to Fd and NAD+ via bif. Hyd to produce Fd2− and NADH. Part of the Fd2− is used by the Rnf complex to produce NADH, which generates a H+ gradient arcoss the cytoplasmic membrane. Additionally, part of the NADH is used by the Stn complex to produce NADPH. Eventually, the above produced Fd2−, NADH and NADPH are used as reducing equivalents in the WLP to catalyze the reduction of CO2 to formate by the formate dehydrogenase (fdh), methenyl-THF to methylene-THF by the methylene-THF dehydrogenase (folD), and methylene-THF to methyl-THF by the MTHFR subunit MetVF, respectively (Schuchmann and Müller, 2014). The bif. Hyd not only provides power for thermodynamically unfavourable reactions but also conserves valuable cellular ATP (Müller et al., 2018). The Stn complex, another long-hidden energy-coupling mechanism based on flavin electron bifurcation, favors the survival of acetogens in energy-limited environments (Wang et al., 2010; Kumar et al., 2023). The electron bifurcation can conserve valuable cellular ATP and combine CO2 fixation in the WLP with ATP synthesis (Lubner et al., 2017; Buckel and Thauer, 2018; Müller et al., 2018). The results indicated that NCDs1:1 modification increased the transmembrane H+ gradient by enhancing Rnf complex-dependent energy conversion process in electric-autotrophic S. ovata, thereby promoting the ATP synthesis required for bacterial growth in energy-limited environments.

The cytochrome/quinone-dependent respiratory chain is a novel energy conversion mechanism in S. ovata. The WLP is loosely connected to the cytoplasmic membrane via the MTHFR subunit MvhD, leading to a direct connection between the cytochrome/quinone-dependent respiratory chain and the carbon flow in WLP (Kremp et al., 2022). As shown in Fig. 5A, the hydrogenase (hupSL and hydA2B2) and cytochrome b (cybH and SOV_3c02070) were upregulated in electro-autotrophic S. ovata with NCDs1:1 modification. Cytochrome b is the membrane anchor of the hydrogenase (Dobrindt and Blaut, 1996; Dross et al., 1992), and its encoding genes may be cybH and SOV_3c02070. HupSLC and HydA2B2C2 are putative membrane-associated, cytochrome b-containing hydrogenases in S. ovata. HupSL is a periplasmic [NiFe] hydrogenase, and HydA2B2 is a periplasmic [FeFe] hydrogenase. As shown in Fig. 5D, electrons are shuttled to ubiquinone (Q) via cytochrome b, and subsequently transferred from ubiquinol (QH2) and MvhD to HdrA or from HdrCB to MetV via the bifurcating flavin of HdrA (Kremp et al., 2022). HdrCBA may drive the endergonic reduction of methylene-THF with QH2 as the electron donor, simultaneously establishing a transmembrane H+ gradient (Rosenbaum and Müller, 2021). H+ generated by the reaction catalyzed by membrane-associated, cytochrome b-containing hydrogenases, along with H+ translocated by the Q cycle, is utilized as the driving force for ATP synthesis. The above process resulted in higher ATP yields than that of the Rnf complex-driven H+ pump.

In summary, NCDs1:1 modification can enhance microbial CO2 fixation and metabolic energy supply by increasing the two H+- pumping step in electric-autotrophic S. ovata, thereby alleviating limited ATP production with higher electron recovery efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hypothesis of EEU mechanisms in NCDs-modified acetogens
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 6 , the reduction of CO2 to acetate requires 8 electrons that might be provided by cathode or molecular hydrogen. For EEU pathway in Sporomusa, cathodic electrons may be transferred from multi-heme cytochrome c to membrane-bound cytochrome bd and/or cytochrome c. Subsequently, electrons are shuttled from Q and/or undefined transmembrane complexes into WLP for CO2 reduction to acetate. This cytochrome/quinone-dependent respiratory chain is directly involved in carbon flow in the WLP via the membrane-associated MvhD and HdrCBA (Kremp et al., 2022). In fact, electrons extracted from water at the anode were transferred to the cathode at −0.4 V (vs. SHE), which is considerably higher than the −0.6 V required to produce low levels of H2 (Nevin et al., 2010; Tremblay et al., 2019). However, undefined redox metabolites released by acetogens may reduce the electrical energy required for H2 evolution in MESs, enabling H2 to be produced at higher cathodic potentials such as −0.4 V (vs. SHE) (Tremblay et al., 2019). Thus, membrane-anchored hydrogenases oxidized H2 in the periplasm, leading to the release of electrons, which may then be transferred from putative cytochrome b and/or Q to the MTHFR in the WLP. Moreover, electrons released from H2 oxidation by cytoplasmic bif. Hyd are shuttled through the redox carriers such as NAD+, NADP+ and/or Fd; and these redox carriers are simultaneously catalyzed by bif. Hyd, Rnf, and Stn to reducing equivalent (Fd2−, NADH, and NADPH) for CO2 reduction in the WLP (Kremp et al., 2020). MESs have broad application prospects in energy conversion and storage, environmental protection, and industrial chemical production. Such enhanced EEU processes in the materials-biology hybrid will enable MESs to move towards carbon neutrality, green economy and sustainable development. In the future research, proteomics for outer/inner membrane fractions is needed to identify the role of cytochrome in the EEU of acetogens.

This study demonstrated that the current generation and acetate accumulation in the MESs had a strong positive correlation with the electrocatalytic activity of NCDs. NCDs1:1, with the highest electrocatalytic activity, obtained the best performance of MESs. NCDs1:1 selectively enriched Sporomusa on the cathodic biofilm. The transcriptional analysis confirmed that cytochromes played an indispensable role in the EEU of Sporomusa strain with NCDs1:1 modification. NCDs1:1 enhanced EEU efficiency, thereby increasing two H+-pumping steps and accelerating microbial CO2 fixation and metabolic energy supply. These results provide insights into understanding the EEU mechanisms by acetogens and increasing CO2 fixation in MESs.

Jiaping Hu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Cuiping Zeng: Writing – review & editing. Guangli Liu: Writing – review & editing. Haiping Luo: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 42077286), the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (Nos. 2023A1515011846, 2023A1515030212). We thank Dr. Yifeng Zhang for the valuable discussions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2024.131390.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
